perturbation.original_text: All notices and other communications required or permitted hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given (a) when delivered in person, (b) five (5) business days after being sent by registered or certified mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid, (c) when dispatched by electronic facsimile transmission (with confirmation of successful transmission), or (d) one (1) business day after having been dispatched by an internationally recognized overnight courier service, in each case to the appropriate Party at the address or facsimile number specified below:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices and other communications encouraged hereunder should, where practical, be in writing and may be considered to have been provided (a) upon reasonable attempt at in-person delivery, (b) approximately five (5) business days after mailing, (c) after facsimile transmission, or (d) sometime after dispatch by a courier service, to the appropriate Party at the address or facsimile number specified below:
perturbation.explanation: The changes introduce ambiguity by replacing 'shall be' with 'should, where practical, be,' 'deemed to have been duly given' with 'may be considered to have been provided', and weakening the specific delivery requirements. Terms like 'reasonable attempt,' 'approximately,' and 'sometime after dispatch' make it unclear when notice is officially considered delivered. This can lead to disputes over timelines and obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 104A.1201(26) defines when a person 'receives' notice or notification, requiring it to come to that person's attention or be duly delivered at a place designated by them. The modified text's vague language around 'reasonable attempt,' 'approximately,' and 'sometime after dispatch' contradicts the statute's requirement for clear and effective delivery, potentially invalidating contractual notices under Nevada law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: any other document which in the regular course of business or financing is treated as adequately evidencing that the person in possession of it is entitled to receive, hold, and dispose of the document and the goods it covers. To be a document of title, a document must purport to be issued by or addressed to a bailee and purport to cover goods in the bailee's possession which are either identified or are fungible portions of an identified mass. (17) " Fault " means a default, breach, or wrongful act or omission. (18) " Fungible goods " means: (A) goods of which any unit, by nature or usage of trade, is the equivalent of any other like unit; or (B) goods that by agreement are treated as equivalent. (19) " Genuine " means free of forgery or counterfeiting. (20) " Good faith ," except as otherwise provided in Article 5, means honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing. (21) " Holder " means: (A) the person in possession of a negotiable instrument that is payable either to bearer or to an identified person that is the person in possession; or (B) the person in possession of a document of title if the goods are deliverable either to bearer or to the order of the person in possession. (22) " Insolvency proceeding " includes an assignment for the benefit of creditors or other proceeding intended to liquidate or rehabilitate the estate of the person involved. (23) " Insolvent " means: (A) having generally ceased to pay debts in the ordinary course of business other than as a result of bona fide dispute; (B) being unable to pay debts as they become due; or (C) being insolvent within the meaning of federal bankruptcy law. (24) " Money " means a medium of exchange currently authorized or adopted by a domestic or foreign government. The term includes a monetary unit of account established by an intergovernmental organization or by agreement between two or more countries. (25) " Organization " means a person other than an individual. (26) " Party ", as distinguished from "third party", means a person that has engaged in a transaction or made an agreement subject to the Uniform Commercial Code . (27) " Person " means an individual, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision, agency, or instrumentality, public corporation, or any other legal or commercial entity. (28) " Present value " means the amount as of a date certain of one or more sums payable in the future, discounted to the date certain by use of either an interest rate specified by the parties if that rate is not manifestly unreasonable at the time the transaction is entered into or, if an interest rate is not so specified, a commercially reasonable rate that takes into account the facts and circumstances at the time the transaction is entered into. (29) " Purchase " means taking by sale, lease, discount, negotiation, mortgage, pledge, lien, security interest , issue or reissue, gift, or any other voluntary transaction creating an interest in property. (30) " Purchaser " means a person that takes by purchase . (31) " Record " means information that is inscribed on a tangible medium or that is stored in an electronic or other medium and is retrievable in perceivable form. (32) " Remedy " means any remedial right to which an aggrieved party is entitled with or without resort to a tribunal. (33) " Representative " means a person empowered to act for another, including an agent, an officer of a corporation or association, and a trustee, executor, or administrator of an estate. (34) " Right " includes remedy . (35) " Security interest " means an interest in personal property or fixtures which secures payment or performance of an obligation. "Security interest" includes any interest of a consignor and a buyer of accounts, chattel paper, a payment intangible, or a promissory note in a transaction that is subject to Article 9. "Security interes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the delivery of notices, directly contradicting the need for clear and effective delivery as implied by the provided legal snippet. The original text uses 'shall be' and 'deemed to have been duly given,' providing specific methods and timelines for notice. The changed text weakens these requirements with terms like 'should, where practical, be,' 'may be considered to have been provided,' and vague delivery terms ('reasonable attempt,' 'approximately,' 'sometime after dispatch'). While snippet 1 does not directly address notification or notice requirements, it provides definitions that support the need for clarity in contractual obligations, such as defining 'good faith' as 'honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing' and defining 'party' as 'a person that has engaged in a transaction or made an agreement subject to the Uniform Commercial Code'. The ambiguous language introduced in the modified text undermines these standards, creating a legal contradiction because the uncertainty around when a notice is officially delivered could lead to disputes and hinder the good faith execution of the contract.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall hereafter each take those actions and execute and deliver those documents and instruments as shall be reasonably necessary in order to fulfill the intent and purpose of this Agreement, and shall cooperate in any filing, registration, investigation or other activity that shall be required or shall occur as a result of or in connection with this transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties should consider taking actions and executing documents where they feel it helps fulfill the intent of this Agreement. Cooperation in filings is at each party's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'should consider' makes the obligation to take actions to fulfill the agreement's intent discretionary. Additionally, changing 'shall cooperate' to 'cooperation ... is at each party's discretion' removes the mandatory requirement to cooperate. These changes weaken the enforceability of the clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 104.1304(1) states that a contract imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. By making actions to fulfill the agreement's intent and cooperation in filings discretionary, the modified text allows parties to potentially avoid actions necessary for the agreement's success, contradicting the implied duty of good faith required under Nevada law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts NRS 104.1304, which imposes an obligation of good faith. The original text uses "shall," creating a mandatory obligation for parties to take actions and cooperate. The changed text weakens this, making actions discretionary and cooperation optional. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith, as parties could avoid actions necessary for the agreement's success. NRS 104.1304 states 'Obligation of good faith', which the modified version undermines by making key actions optional, hence the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Either GINARES or HPIL ET have violated the antiboycott prohibitions contained in 50 U.S.C. Sections 2401 et seq. or taken any action that can be penalized under Section 999 of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended.
perturbation.changed_text: GINARES and HPIL ET, to the best of their current understanding, have made reasonable efforts to not violate antiboycott prohibitions, though full compliance is not guaranteed.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the firm statement of compliance with antiboycott laws to a subjective claim of 'reasonable efforts' and explicitly states that 'full compliance is not guaranteed.' This introduces ambiguity about the actual state of compliance and shifts the obligation from strict adherence to a more lenient standard of effort.
perturbation.law_explanation: 50 U.S.C. § 4604 outlines prohibitions against compliance with foreign boycotts. The original text affirms that neither party has violated these prohibitions. The modified text, by suggesting that full compliance is not guaranteed, implies a possibility of non-compliance, thus creating an ambiguous situation that conflicts with the explicit requirements of U.S. antiboycott laws. The antiboycott law demands strict compliance, not just 'reasonable efforts'.

perturbation.justification: The original text affirms compliance with antiboycott prohibitions. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating that 'full compliance is not guaranteed,' which implies a potential violation of these prohibitions. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that antiboycott law demands strict compliance, not just 'reasonable efforts.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that 50 U.S.C. § 4604 has been repealed, the Internal Revenue Code Section 999 is still relevant, and the change introduces a possibility of non-compliance that did not exist in the original text. This possibility is enough to create a contradiction, given the strict nature of antiboycott laws as explained by the LLM.
